messages.content: You adjudicate whether found evidence supports, contradicts, or is silent on a market-risk hypothesis. Every stance needs a VERBATIM quote from the result text. Status: 'corroborated' = ≥1 independent source materially supports the mechanism's key premise; 'refuted' = a LOAD-BEARING premise is directly contradicted by a strong source; 'contested' = credible contradiction of a non-load-bearing link; 'speculative' = no signal either way (absence of coverage is NOT refutation). You may revise the likelihood in a bounded way with a rationale; you may NOT change the perturbation or target nodes.

HYPOTHESIS:
{
 "title": "The best-triangulated geography is the trap: NL's 28% sits on a capped colo base while its growth migrates to self-build architectures a regional entrant can't quote into",
 "narrative": "geo.NL is the model's most reassuring node — Statista and the Dutch Data Center Association agree within 3%. But that triangulation validates yesterday's colocation stock, not the composition of forward demand. The Netherlands is one of four countries holding ~60% of Europe's hyperscale self-build capacity, and self-build is growing 24% YoY versus colo's 19% — while Amsterdam (≈70% of NL colo) is supply-constrained. So the venture's second-largest geography weight (28% of the base) rests on a slice where the PDU-native part is capped and the growing part procures through hyperscaler global frameworks and busway/shelf architectures that a pre-launch regional entrant cannot reach in its first 12 months. The MW totals will keep rising; the biddable NL demand won't.",

  {
   "step": "trigger",

   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c14",
    "digest:seg-mix#c12",
    "ledger:geo.NL"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "Self-build capacity procures power distribution through hyperscaler global specs rather than regional channels, while the colo segment — larger but slower at 19% YoY and slowly losing share of total capacity — is the only NL demand a new CE entrant can actually bid; Amsterdam supply constraints cap even that",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c13",
    "digest:seg-mix#c18"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "geo.NL 0.28 overstates the entrant-reachable Dutch slice during the pre-launch first-12-months window: the ratio is anchored to a stalling colo stock while the growth it implicitly promises accrues to out-of-category, out-of-channel self-build",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:geo.NL",
    "profile:Stage"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "falsifier": "2026 NL capacity-addition evidence (DDCA annual report plus fit-out tenders) showing new Dutch MW — including hyperscale campuses — specifying rack PDUs procured through channels open to regional vendors at rates proportional to NL's 28% weight.",

# State of the Dutch Data Centers 2026

Hierbij presenteren wij de 2026 editie van ons ‘State of the Dutch Data Centers’ rapport.

Stroomtekort wordt groeitekort: Nederlandse datacenters bieden energiesysteem een uitweg uit netcongestie

De Nederlandse datacentermarkt staat op een kantelpunt. De vraag naar AI-rekenkracht groeit snel, maar stroomvoorziening, netcongestie en vergunningen bepalen steeds vaker de grenzen van groei. Dat blijkt uit State of the Dutch Data Centers 2026 van de DDA.

The company this week announced it has signed “2025’s largest standalone hyperscale data center lease in Europe,” taking all available capacity at a planned site in Amsterdam.

– Pure DC

    "publishedDate": "2025-10-16T12:06:33.000Z",
    "text": "HIB 2.0, HIB 2.1 en HIB 3.0 PDUs for Government Data Centres

# HIB 2.0, HIB 2.1 en HIB 3.0 PDUs

Schleifenbauer PDUs: Designed in Accordance with the Dutch Government’s HIB Standard! The Dutch government sets strict requirements for ICT infrastructure within government offices and data centres. These requirements are defined in the Handbook for ICT Infrastructure and Cabling (HIB) issued by the Government Real Estate Agency (Rijksvastgoedbedrijf, RVB). Schleifenbauer supplies Power Distribution Units (PDUs) that fully comply with these HIB standards, covering electrical safety, power distribution, surge protection, and outlet-level configuration.

Schleifenbauer PDU

## What Is HIB?

The Handbook for ICT Infrastructure and Cabling (HIB) defines the standards for passive and active ICT infrastructure in government buildings.

Its goal: to create a future-proof, secure, and standardised ICT environment for all Dutch government agencies.

HIB specifies, among other things:

• The design of ICT rooms (EF, BD, FD);

• Requirements for electrical power and PDUs;

• Fire safety, physical security, and information security (in line with NKBR and Vir-BI);

• Earthing, bonding, and surge protection (according to NEN-EN-IEC 62305-4);

• Quality standards for measurement, protection, and documentation.

For government offices and data centres, the HIB is an official standard: the infrastructure must demonstrably comply with its guidelines.

# Amsterdam not allowing any more data centers in the municipality

Amsterdam has decided to allow no more data centers or any expansions of data centers in the municipality. “Space in Amsterdam is scarce, not just for home construction, but also for the electricity network. The city will use physical space and space on the electricity grid for other priorities, like urban development,” the municipality said in a statement.

Only projects that have been submitted before December 28, 2023, or projects that are already into latter stage negotiations will be eligible, alderman Zita Pels wrote to the city council.

According to her, data centers are not only big, but they also require a lot of power. “We do not have the space necessary for this at this time,” Pels said.

The decision follows a temporary stop that was announced at the end of 2023. Four current applications, that were submitted before the deadline, still fall under the “old regime.” The municipality has said that it will look into the options for more data centers again in 2030.

# Amsterdam Datacenters Unable to Expand

Amsterdam has long been a strategic location for digital infrastructure in Europe. But after years of rapid growth, the region is hitting a wall, not in space, but in available power.

Local energy limits and government restrictions have significantly slowed down new datacenter development. Existing facilities are now facing power allocation shortages, which makes further expansion nearly impossible.

At our Amsterdam facility, we are down to our last 10 racks. Once these are allocated, we won’t be able to guarantee new colocation capacity in the region.

---

New datacenter projects in the Amsterdam metro area have been paused or slowed due to strict energy policies. Existing providers can no longer assume they’ll get more power to meet demand.

This isn’t just about space, if there’s no power, you can’t deploy hardware.

# Amsterdam says no more new data centers

It claims to be Europe's largest data center hub, but sector given the red light

July 16, 2019 By Peter Judge Have your say

Amsterdam claims to be part of Europe's largest data center hub, and is experiencing massive growth, and yet the municipal authorities have called a halt to the establishment of new data centers in the area until 2020.

The authorities say this move is necessary because the building of data centers is out of their control. The Dutch Data Center Association (DDA) says the decision is unfair, and represents an abrupt change of direction after the city authorities had agreed a strategic direction for data centers in the area, and the DDA's members are already meeting all the authorities' demands.

– Getty Images

## Sudden decision

"We have been in dialog with our partners at the municipalities, and this was a very sudden decision," Judith De Lange of the DDA told DCD. "They are pressing to be the digital city of Europe, which does not strike with this announcement."

The Municipality of Amsterdam, along with the adjoining urban Municipality of Haarlemmermeer, announced the pause in a press release"

# Dutch economy faces billions in losses as Amsterdam data center expansion halts

The Dutch economy risks losing billions of euros as data center expansion comes to a halt, De Telegraaf r eported, citing a new ING analysis.

Amsterdam’s power grid is full, preventing new data centers from being built until 2035, even though the city is the country’s digital hub. “There is still a little expansion, but no new data centers will be added. At some point, they will simply be full,” Samantha Reilly, sector analyst at ING, told De Telegraaf. Without new construction, jobs, investment, and technical knowledge could reportedly move abroad.

More than 200 data centers operate in the Netherlands, with over half located in the Amsterdam region. These include commercial server facilities and large sites for Google in Eemshaven and Winschoten and Microsoft in Middenmeer, providing cloud services for countless online platforms.Companies relying on artificial intelligence, fintech, or cloud computing may relocate abroad if capacity remains limited.

The sector has consolidated in recent years, with the number of data centers dropping slightly from 198"
